paragraphs.body: In Bird by Bird, Anne Lamott writes, “Characters should not, conversely, serve as pawns for some plot you’ve dreamed up,” emphasizing that the best stories come from the characters, not pre-planned plot points. She wants to make it clear that the plot should emerge from the characters, not the other way around. This idea challenged the way I viewed storytelling. At first, I created characters to fit into the events I had already imagined. I treated the characters as tools to move the plot forward. This caused me to neglect character development. Lamott helped me understand that good fiction starts with people, who they are, what they want, and what they are struggling with. A good story has characters first who eventually create a plot through their choices. The plot shouldn’t just come out of nowhere; it should make sense due to the character’s personality and emotions. If a character’s choices and reactions feel real, the story will also feel more real.

annotations.comment: Great quote to choose — I really like that one.

annotations.text: This idea challenged the way I viewed storytelling.
annotations.comment: Yes, it’s often completely opposite what you’ve been taught!

paragraphs.body: In “The Haunted House”, a student’s story by , Amelia’s tragic plot is determined by her emotional struggles and decisions. Her driving to the abandoned house and using drugs are actions that shape the story and the result of her trauma. Rather than feeling like the plot is leading her actions, it is clear that the character’s pain is what drives her to make decisions. The events don’t feel forced or convenient, they feel inevitable based on the character. That is what makes the story so effective, because it doesn’t rely on plot twists but on the characters’ emotional state. This makes the story feel more realistic.

I noticed an effective building of the plot while reading “Rubberdust” by Sarah Thankam Mathews from Best American Short Stories 2020. Similarly to “The Haunted House,” this story centers around personal experiences and emotions rather than an external plot to drive the action. The plot unfolds because of how the characters interact and their internal struggles. The conflict between the protagonist and Anuj develops naturally due to their personalities. The protagonist is quiet, lonely, and impressionable, while Anuj is bolder and attention-seeking. This dynamic leads them to drive the plot and bully Karan. Later, the protagonist’s guilt guides her actions, leading her to apologize and try and make things right. This is a great example of the internal struggles of the character, furthering the narrative and creating the emotional arc of the story. Like Lamott suggests, the plot isn’t something imposed, but something that comes from the messiness of real human emotions.

I tried to apply this to my own stories, making the characters have a backstory that eventually leads to events unfolding. For example, in “The Perfect Girlfriend,” my first short story, I wanted David’s loneliness and his need for human affection to guide his actions and create conflict. Additionally, I wanted Ivy’s robotic and soulless nature to add to the plot. Originally, I focused more on what I wanted to happen and not who David and Ivy were. Focusing on their personalities and emotional needs allows the story to flow a lot more naturally. I thought that David’s yearning for connection and Ivy’s inability to give him real love created organic tension. I think Lamott’s ideas on plot truly help to make stories better and more realistic.

annotations.text: Originally, I focused more on what I wanted to happen and not who David and Ivy were
annotations.comment: It’s hard not to, but I think your second draft shows that you were able to start listening to them more.

paragraphs.body: One thing I struggled with while writing fiction was creating authentic dialogue that felt natural and helped develop characters. Anne Lamott writes in Bird by Bird, “One line of dialogue that rings true reveals character in a way that pages of description can’t.” Just one single, well-crafted line of dialogue can convey a lot about a character’s essence and personality. This can make the character feel more authentic. This advice made me realize my faults in writing dialogue. I usually rely on narration to describe and explain characters, but I see now how powerful it is when a character reveals themselves by speaking. It is not just about what they said but also how they said it and what they choose to leave unsaid. As Lamott says, the dialogue should also be true to each character and reflect their unique personality. Each person should sound different, because that is how people sound in real life.

annotations.comment: Yes, although that is hard to write!

paragraphs.body: I noticed good use of dialogue in the short story “The Haunted House” by . The tension between the family members is shown mostly through emotionally loaded lines of dialogue. For example, when Andrea says, “But to overdose in the same house where our dad killed himself? God, she is the most selfish person that…” Just this line shows us about her anger, her pain, her judgment, and the grief the family still feels for their dad. It tells us more than a full paragraph or description would have. I also saw effective dialogue in “Rubberdust” by Sarah Thankam Mathews from Best American Short Stories 2020. Being children, I think the characters sound young, and their voices fit the identity of a child. This aligns with Lamott’s ideas that the characters’ dialogue should be authentic to them. Anuj’s bolder nature and the protagonist’s shy, curious personality are captured within their dialogue. This makes the characters in the story feel more real and relatable.

In my second short story, “The WishMaker,” I used very few lines of dialogue to convey complex ideas. For example, when Nicole’s mother scolds her for being out late, it shows the tension between them and the mother’s harsh nature. Nicole’s passive response, “My phone died. I was just at Lauren’s party, which I told you about yesterday,” demonstrates her reluctance to engage in a conflict with her mom. These three lines of dialogue show Nicole’s fear and how she is emotionally distant from her mother. It also illustrates how her mom is controlling over her, which makes the storyline of her wishing to be an adult make more sense. Similarly, in my first flash fiction story, “The Perfect Girlfriend,” I used Lamott’s strategies to create effective dialogue. Ivy has a more robotic and unnatural way of speaking to fit her character of being an AI girlfriend. She doesn’t use contractions, and her responses seem less emotional than David’s. In David’s dialogue, I wanted to emphasize his loneliness and longing for connection. For example, when he says, “This past year has been the happiest of my life,” it is supposed to emphasize how he has lacked connection so far in life. In both stories, the dialogue is used not only to drive the plot forward but to deepen the understanding of the characters’ emotions and struggles.

annotations.comment: It’s helpful to read this! A lovely paper, Jayden. Thank you for all of your hard work! Grade: A

paragraphs.body: Throughout my journey during this class, I have come to understand the effects authentic, effective dialogue and character-driven plots have on storytelling. Lamott’s ideas helped me reshape my writing. I now know to allow characters to shape the plot when writing fiction. I now see that dialogue isn’t just about furthering the plot, but a way to show the soul of a character. I believe that by embracing Lamott’s approach, I can write more compelling fiction.
